questions.assertion: Assertion (A): When a body is at rest, the net force acting on it is zero.
questions.reason: Reason (R): According to Newton's first law of motion, a body at rest remains at rest, and a body in motion remains in motion unless acted upon by an external force.
opts: (A) Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
(B) Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
(C) A is true but R is false
(D) A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Newton's first law of motion explains why a body at rest remains at rest when no external force is acting on it.

questions.assertion: Assertion (A): The acceleration of an object depends on the net force acting on it.
questions.reason: Reason (R): According to Newton's second law of motion, force (F) is equal to the rate of change of momentum (F = dp/dt), and since acceleration (a) is the rate of change of velocity, it depends on the net force acting on the object.
opts: (A) Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
(B) Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
(C) A is true but R is false
(D) A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Newton's second law of motion explains why acceleration depends on the net force acting on an object.

questions.assertion: Assertion (A): Frictional force always acts in the direction opposite to that of the applied force.
questions.reason: Reason (R): Frictional force opposes the motion of an object, and since it always acts in the opposite direction, it opposes the force applied on the object.
opts: (A) Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
(B) Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
(C) A is true but R is false
(D) A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Frictional force always opposes the motion of an object, making it act in the opposite direction.

questions.assertion: Assertion (A): An object can have zero acceleration even if a force is acting on it.
questions.reason: Reason (R): According to Newton's second law of motion, force (F) is equal to mass (m) times acceleration (a) (F = ma), and if the force is balanced by an equal and opposite force, the net force acting on the object will be zero, resulting in zero acceleration.
opts: (A) Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
(B) Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
(C) A is true but R is false
(D) A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Newton's second law of motion explains why an object can have zero acceleration even if a force is acting on it.

questions.assertion: Assertion (A): The force of friction is independent of the surface area of contact between the two surfaces.
questions.reason: Reason (R): According to Newton's third law of motion, forces always occur in pairs - equal and opposite action-reaction force pairs, and since the force of friction is a result of the normal force acting on the object, it is independent of the surface area of contact.
opts: (A) Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
(B) Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
(C) A is true but R is false
(D) A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: The force of friction is actually dependent on the surface area of contact between the two surfaces.

questions.assertion: Assertion (A): The inertia of an object depends on its mass.
questions.reason: Reason (R): According to Newton's first law of motion, the inertia of an object is directly proportional to its mass, and since mass is a measure of the amount of matter in an object, it directly affects the object's inertia.
opts: (A) Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
(B) Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
(C) A is true but R is false
(D) A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Newton's first law of motion explains why the inertia of an object depends on its mass.

questions.assertion: Assertion (A): The force of gravity acts on all objects with the same intensity, regardless of their mass.
questions.reason: Reason (R): According to Newton's law of gravitation, the force of gravity (F) is proportional to the product of the masses (m1 and m2) of the two objects and inversely proportional to the square of the distance (r) between their centers (F = G(m1m2)/r^2), and since the mass of an object affects the intensity of the force of gravity, it is not true that the force of gravity acts on all objects with the same intensity.
opts: (A) Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
(B) Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
(C) A is true but R is false
(D) A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: The force of gravity does not act on all objects with the same intensity, as it depends on the mass of the objects and the distance between them.

questions.assertion: Assertion (A): The force of friction is directly proportional to the normal force acting on an object.
questions.reason: Reason (R): According to Newton's third law of motion, forces always occur in pairs - equal and opposite action-reaction force pairs, and since the force of friction is a result of the normal force acting on the object, it is directly proportional to the normal force.
opts: (A) Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
(B) Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
(C) A is true but R is false
(D) A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: The force of friction is indeed directly proportional to the normal force acting on an object.

questions.assertion: Assertion (A): The acceleration of an object is independent of its mass.
questions.reason: Reason (R): According to Newton's second law of motion, force (F) is equal to mass (m) times acceleration (a) (F = ma), and since force is a measure of the rate of change of momentum, it is not independent of mass.
opts: (A) Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
(B) Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
(C) A is true but R is false
(D) A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: The acceleration of an object depends on its mass, as explained by Newton's second law of motion.
